questions.question: A packet arrives and the 2-byte field at byte offset 12 reads 0x0028 (decimal 40). How should an Ethernet parser classify this frame?
options: Ethernet II with EtherType 0x0028
IEEE 802.3 frame; the field is a Length value indicating 40 bytes of payload
Reserved — the value falls in the forbidden 1501–1535 range
VLAN-tagged — the parser should read 4 bytes further for the real EtherType
questions.explanation: Values ≤ 1500 (0x05DC) are interpreted as Length fields under the IEEE 802.3 standard, not EtherTypes. Decimal 40 (0x0028) is well within that range, so the frame is IEEE 802.3 with 40 bytes of payload following the LLC header.

questions.question: Why must values in the range 1501–1535 cause a frame to be discarded rather than parsed as either Ethernet II or IEEE 802.3?
options: They indicate the frame uses jumbo (over-sized) Ethernet frames that require special hardware
No IEEE standard assigns Length or EtherType semantics to those values, so the frame format is undefined and the frame must be treated as invalid
They are reserved for future 802.1Q VLAN extensions
They exceed the 1500-byte MTU of Fast Ethernet and would cause buffer overflows
questions.explanation: Values 1501–1535 are not assigned by any standard as either a valid payload Length (max 1500) or a valid EtherType (min 1536). A compliant parser must discard any frame whose Length/Type field falls in this reserved range.

questions.question: A received frame has the following bytes starting at offset 12: 81 00 00 64 08 00. What is the VLAN ID and the inner EtherType?
options: VLAN ID = 0, EtherType = 0x8100 (another VLAN tag)
VLAN ID = 100 (0x0064 & 0x0FFF), inner EtherType = 0x0800 (IPv4)
VLAN ID = 25600 (0x6400), inner EtherType = 0x0800
VLAN ID = 100, inner EtherType = 0x8100 (nested 802.1Q)
questions.explanation: 0x8100 is the 802.1Q TPID. The next 2 bytes (0x0064 = 100) form the TCI; masking with 0x0FFF gives VLAN ID 100. The parser then re-reads the next 2 bytes (0x0800) as the inner EtherType, which identifies IPv4.

questions.question: An IEEE 802.3 frame has DSAP=0xAA and SSAP=0xAA. What does this combination signal, and what are the next 5 bytes?
options: The LLC control field uses I-format (information frame) and the next 5 bytes are sequence numbers
DSAP=0xAA/SSAP=0xAA is the SNAP SAP; the 5 bytes that follow are a 3-byte OUI plus a 2-byte Protocol ID
DSAP=0xAA/SSAP=0xAA means the frame carries an ARP payload; the next 5 bytes are the hardware type
The 802.3 frame is invalid because 0xAA is not a registered SAP value
questions.explanation: The value 0xAA in both DSAP and SSAP is the SNAP (SubNetwork Access Protocol) SAP. It signals that a 5-byte SNAP extension follows immediately after the 3-byte LLC header: 3 bytes of OUI (Organization Unique Identifier) and 2 bytes of Protocol ID, allowing 802.3 frames to carry any EtherType-identified protocol.

questions.question: A QinQ frame has its outer tag with TPID=0x88A8 and TCI=0x00C8, and its inner tag with TPID=0x8100 and TCI=0x0064. What are the SVLAN and CVLAN IDs?
options: SVLAN=200, CVLAN=100
SVLAN=100, CVLAN=200
SVLAN=0x88A8, CVLAN=0x8100
SVLAN=0xC8, CVLAN=0x64
questions.explanation: The outer S-tag (IEEE 802.1ad, TPID=0x88A8) carries SVLAN=0x00C8 & 0x0FFF = 200. The inner C-tag (802.1Q, TPID=0x8100) carries CVLAN=0x0064 & 0x0FFF = 100. The parser appends VLAN IDs in order encountered: [200, 100].

questions.question: During FCS verification the parser computes CRC-32 over all frame bytes except the last 4, then compares the result to the last 4 bytes stored in the frame. The last 4 bytes are read in which byte order, and why?
options: Big-endian (network byte order), to be consistent with all other Ethernet header fields
Little-endian, because the Ethernet FCS is transmitted and stored LSB-first as defined by IEEE 802.3
Little-endian only on x86 hosts; the byte order is architecture-dependent
The CRC is XOR-masked with 0xFFFFFFFF before comparison, so byte order is irrelevant
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.3 specifies that the FCS is transmitted least-significant bit first, meaning the 32-bit CRC value is stored in little-endian byte order at the end of the frame. The parser must use struct.unpack('<I', frame[-4:]) (little-endian unsigned int) to correctly read the on-wire FCS before comparing it to the recomputed value.
